Work Sample
This document demonstrates how Lucent Prism designs a documentation programme — not a single document, but the governed system that produces, sustains, and evolves documentation as the organisation grows.
1
Part One

Programme Architecture

The difference between a documentation project and a documentation programme

You have asked for documents before. You received them. Six months later, nobody uses them, nobody maintains them, and you are back where you started — except you have spent money. The problem was never the document. It was the absence of the system designed to produce, govern, and sustain it.

"The document is the byproduct. The system that produces it, governs it, and keeps it alive — that is the asset."

A documentation project ends when the document is delivered. A documentation programme never ends — it governs how knowledge is captured, structured, maintained, and used as the organisation itself evolves. This framework describes how that programme is designed, built, and sustained.

1
Governance Layer
Who owns what. Who approves what. How standards are set and enforced. The accountability structure that makes everything else sustainable.
2
Architecture Layer
How knowledge is structured. What domains need coverage. What document types belong where. The information architecture that precedes any writing.
3
Production Layer
How documents are created. The standards, templates, and quality gates that ensure consistency regardless of who writes or when.
4
Sustainability Layer
How the programme stays alive. Review cycles, version control, ownership transfers, and feedback loops that keep documentation current and trusted.
Why Most Documentation Efforts Fail

They invest in the production layer without building the other three. Excellent documents are produced, filed, ignored, and outdated within a year. The writing was never the problem. The absence of governance, architecture, and sustainability was.

2
Part Two

Maturity-Calibrated Design

The programme design depends entirely on where the organisation is now

There is no universal documentation programme. Designing the wrong programme for the wrong stage is as costly as having no programme at all. An L1 organisation given an L4 programme will not use it. An L3 organisation given an L1 programme will outgrow it in months. Every LP engagement begins with an intake assessment that positions the organisation on this scale — and the programme design follows from that position, not from what sounds comprehensive.

LevelState of the OrganisationObservable SignalProgramme Priority
L1
Informal

Processes exist in people's heads. When a key person leaves, knowledge leaves with them.

New hires take 8–12 weeks to learn how things are done. The same questions are asked repeatedly. Outcomes depend on who is in the room.

First SOPs and policies. Job descriptions. Onboarding framework. Single source of truth.

L2
Emerging

Core documents exist but are scattered, inconsistent, and ungoverned. Some teams are documented. Others rely on institutional memory.

Documents exist but cannot be found. Multiple versions circulate. Compliance documents are filed but not followed.

Document management system. Naming and version standards. SOP library structure. Policy framework.

L3
Defined

Documentation is governed, approved, consistently followed, and audit-ready. Reviews are scheduled, not reactive.

Any process document can be located within two minutes. New hires receive structured onboarding. Audits are prepared for, not panicked over.

Complete SOP library. Full policy suite. Training documentation. Audit evidence packages.

L4
Measured

Performance is measured against documented standards. Documentation drives continuous improvement rather than merely recording current practice.

SOPs include performance metrics. Review cycles produce documented improvements. Time-to-competency is tracked.

Performance management frameworks. Corrective action documentation. KPI-linked SOPs.

L5
Autonomous

Knowledge is a strategic asset actively managed, continuously improved, and deliberately shared. The organisation learns faster than individuals.

Knowledge transfer is systematic. Time-to-competency is in weeks, not months. Near-misses become learning before they become failures.

Knowledge ecosystem architecture. AI collaboration frameworks. Self-renewing documentation systems.

The Intake Assessment

Every LP engagement begins with a structured maturity assessment. The organisation does not need to arrive knowing what it needs. Finding that out is where the work begins.

3
Part Three

Information Architecture Framework

What must be mapped before a word is written

The most expensive documentation mistake is beginning with a document. A brief arrives, writing begins, a document is produced. What was never asked: where does this document sit in the organisation's knowledge landscape? What does it depend on? What does it expose about what is missing around it?

"Most briefs reveal, within the first conversation, that the single document requested is the symptom of a system gap. The audit surfaces that gap before the document compounds it."

Knowledge AreaDiagnostic QuestionsRisk Level
Process Knowledge

Are core operational processes documented? Has practice drifted from the written procedure? When a process fails, is there a documented root cause mechanism?

High where undocumented
Institutional Knowledge

What critical knowledge exists only in specific people's heads? What decisions — and why — inform current practice but are recorded nowhere? What leaves if three key people leave this month?

Critical — exits are irreversible
Governance Knowledge

Are approval authorities documented? Do staff know who can authorise what? Are escalation paths defined in writing, or understood informally?

High in regulated sectors
Compliance Knowledge

What regulatory or accreditation requirements apply? Are those requirements mapped to specific documents? Has the document set been reviewed against the current edition of the relevant standard?

Legal and reputational exposure
Client-Facing Knowledge

Is the client experience consistent regardless of which team member delivers it? Is client-specific knowledge accessible to the whole team, or held by the relationship owner?

Revenue and retention impact
Learning and Development

How does a new hire learn to do the work? Is that a designed, documented experience — or 12 weeks of informal absorption? What is the cost of the current time-to-competency?

Quantifiable productivity loss
Principle

The information architecture audit is not a documentation gap analysis. It is a knowledge risk assessment — identifying where the organisation is most exposed to knowledge loss, inconsistency, and compliance failure. The programme closes those risks in order of priority, not ease.

4
Part Four

Governance and Sustainability

Why programmes succeed or fail after the documents are written

A governance structure is not a bureaucratic layer added on top of documentation work. It is the mechanism that determines whether the programme produces a living system or an expanding archive of documents nobody uses, nobody updates, and nobody trusts.

Every document without an owner becomes outdated. Every system without a review cycle becomes a record of how things used to be done. Governance is the difference between documentation as a project and documentation as an organisational capability.

Governance Principles

Single OwnershipEvery document has one named owner — the person accountable for its accuracy and currency. Not a department. One person.
Scheduled ReviewEvery document has a defined review cycle commensurate with its rate of change. Governance: annual. Operational SOPs: every six months. Regulatory: on standard change.
Version IntegrityOne version of the truth. The current approved version is accessible. Superseded versions are archived, not circulating. Naming conventions enforce this.
Approval AuthorityEvery document tier has a defined approval authority. Documents become effective when approved — not when written.

Document Tiering

TierDocument TypesReview Cycle
Tier 1 — Strategic
Governance frameworks, knowledge policy, programme charter, risk register
Annual · Board or Senior Leadership
Tier 2 — Operational
Core SOPs, client-facing procedures, department policies, cross-functional processes
Every 6 months · Department Head
Tier 3 — Procedural
Task-level procedures, checklists, templates, job aids, quick reference guides
Annual or on process change
Tier 4 — Reference
Policy summaries, FAQs, glossaries, onboarding guides, training materials
Annual · Knowledge Champion
The Most Common Governance Failure

Assigning document ownership to a department rather than a person. Departments do not update documents. People do — when they are accountable, when the expectation is clear, and when the review cycle is built into their calendar, not into a policy nobody reads.

5
Part Five

Implementation Roadmap

Phase-gated delivery from assessment to autonomous operation

Implementation is sequenced by risk, not by ease. The first documents produced are not the simplest to write — they are the ones whose absence creates the greatest organisational exposure. Every phase has defined deliverables and quality gates. The programme does not advance until the gate is satisfied.

"Sequenced by risk, not by ease. The programme does not advance until the gate is satisfied."

0
Phase Zero
Assessment and Architecture

The intake assessment establishes documentation maturity. The information architecture audit maps knowledge risks and priorities. The programme design is calibrated to the organisation's maturity level. No document production begins at Phase Zero. This phase produces the plan — not the documents.

Gate Deliverables
Documentation maturity report
Knowledge risk register
Programme design and scope
Approved document hierarchy
1
Phase One
Governance Foundation

The governance layer is established before any operational documentation begins. Document ownership is assigned. Naming conventions and version control standards are set. The document management system is configured. Without this foundation, every subsequent document is ungoverned.

Gate Deliverables
Document governance policy
Naming and version standards
Document management system
Ownership and approval matrix
2
Phase Two
Critical Knowledge Capture

The highest-risk knowledge gaps identified in Phase Zero are addressed first. Processes held only in people's heads are documented. Institutional knowledge at risk of departure is captured. The documents that protect the organisation are written before the documents that improve it.

Gate Deliverables
Priority SOPs completed
Core policies drafted and approved
Critical knowledge captured
Knowledge risk register updated
3
Phase Three
Operational Documentation

The full operational document set is produced — department by department, function by function. Each document is written to the governance standard established in Phase One, approved by the authority established in Phase One, and filed in the system established in Phase One.

Gate Deliverables
Complete SOP library
Full policy suite
Compliance evidence packages
Training and onboarding materials
4
Phase Four
Review Cycle Activation

The sustainability layer is activated. Review cycles are initiated. Ownership transfers are completed. The programme transitions from a production engagement to a governance engagement. The first review cycle is managed alongside the team that will own all subsequent cycles.

Gate Deliverables
First review cycle complete
Ownership transferred and confirmed
Team trained on governance standards
Programme health metrics established
5
Phase Five
Autonomous Operation

The programme operates independently. The organisation owns the system. Documentation is a governed, self-sustaining capability — not a recurring external engagement. The Knowledge Architect's role at Phase Five is advisory: reviewing governance health and designing the next evolution as the organisation grows.

Gate Deliverables
Programme health dashboard
Annual governance review protocol
Escalation and support framework
Evolution roadmap for next maturity level

"The system is the asset. The document is its byproduct."
